perturbation.original_text: For a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, Investor will not, without the Company’s prior written consent, directly or indirectly, solicit for purposes of employment, offer to hire or offer to enter into any contract with any executive officer of the Company with whom Investor has contact, or of whom Investor otherwise becomes aware, during its discussions with the Company or its evaluation of a possible Transaction; provided, however, that this paragraph shall not prohibit Investor from engagement in any general advertising or general solicitation not targeted to the Company’s employees; and provided, further, that Investor shall not be prohibited from soliciting or hiring any such employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company or its affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of five years from the date of this Agreement, Investor will not, without the Company’s prior written consent, directly or indirectly, solicit for purposes of employment, offer to hire or offer to enter into any contract with any executive officer of the Company with whom Investor has contact, or of whom Investor otherwise becomes aware, during its discussions with the Company or its evaluation of a possible Transaction; provided, however, that this paragraph shall not prohibit Investor from engagement in any general advertising or general solicitation not targeted to the Company’s employees; and provided, further, that Investor shall not be prohibited from soliciting or hiring any such employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company or its affiliates.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be considered an unreasonably long duration, potentially violating California's prohibition on non-compete agreements, which can extend to non-solicitation clauses depending on the circumstances and the scope of the restriction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While non-solicitation clauses are not per se invalid, a five-year restriction could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if it prevents an employee from pursuing legitimate career opportunities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from eighteen months to five years. The Law Explanation suggests that this five-year restriction could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, particularly if it prevents employees from pursuing legitimate career opportunities. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While non-solicitation agreements aren't per se illegal, the extended duration poses a significant risk of being construed as an unreasonable restraint, thus contradicting the statute's broad prohibition.

perturbation.original_text: At any time upon the written request of the Company, Investor will, at its own expense, (i) reasonably promptly redeliver to the Company or, at the at Investor’s option, destroy all copies (including permanently erasing or deleting any electronic copies) of the Evaluation Material in the possession or control of Investor or any of its Representatives and (ii) reasonably promptly destroy all analyses, notes, data, compilations, summaries, forecasts, studies or other documents and materials prepared by Investor or any of its Representatives in connection with their evaluation and review of a possible Transaction that contain, reflect or are based upon or generated from, in whole or in part, any of the Evaluation Material. Upon the Company’s written request, Investor will confirm any such destruction to the Company in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time upon the verbal request of the Company, Investor will, at its own expense, (i) immediately redeliver to the Company or, at the at Investor’s option, destroy all copies (including permanently erasing or deleting any electronic copies) of the Evaluation Material in the possession or control of Investor or any of its Representatives and (ii) immediately destroy all analyses, notes, data, compilations, summaries, forecasts, studies or other documents and materials prepared by Investor or any of its Representatives in connection with their evaluation and review of a possible Transaction that contain, reflect or are based upon or generated from, in whole or in part, any of the Evaluation Material. Upon the Company’s verbal request, Investor will confirm any such destruction to the Company in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written request' to 'verbal request' and 'reasonably promptly' to 'immediately' for the return/destruction of Evaluation Material may violate federal regulations concerning record retention, particularly if the Evaluation Material relates to potential securities violations, business dealings or financial reporting.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 mandates specific record retention requirements for broker-dealers. The regulation necessitates maintaining records for prescribed periods, and altering or destroying these records prematurely based on a verbal request could impede compliance with these obligations and regulatory audits. While the NDA is not directly about a broker-dealer, the evaluation material might contain information that would fall under these record-keeping requirements if the Investor is a broker-dealer.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.17a-4 Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. This section applies to the following types of entities: A member of a national securities exchange who transacts a business in securities directly with others than members of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer who transacts a business in securities through the medium of a member of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer as that term is defined in § 240.3b-12 , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o ); a security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o -10) that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. Section 240.18a-6 (rather than this section) applies to the following types of entities: A security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. (a) Every member , broker or dealer subject to § 240.17a-3 must preserve for a period of not less than 6 years, the first two years
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'written request' with 'verbal request' and 'reasonably promptly' with 'immediately' regarding the return/destruction of Evaluation Material. The Law Explanation states that 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 mandates specific record retention requirements for broker-dealers, and premature alteration or destruction based on a verbal request could impede compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 2 from 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 discusses record preservation requirements for certain exchange members, brokers, and dealers. Although the NDA does not directly involve a broker-dealer, if the Evaluation Material contains information that would fall under these record-keeping requirements (should the Investor be a broker-dealer), then the modified contract text requiring immediate destruction based on a verbal request contradicts the record preservation requirements outlined in the regulation. Changing the time frame to 'immediately' further exacerbates the issue of potential non-compliance with record retention requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Investor acknowledges that it is aware, and that it will advise its Representatives who receive the Evaluation Material, that the United States securities laws prohibit any person who has received from an issuer material, non-public information from purchasing or selling securities of such issuer (and options, warrants and rights relating thereto) or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: Investor acknowledges that it is aware, and that it will not advise its Representatives who receive the Evaluation Material, that the United States securities laws prohibit any person who has received from an issuer material, non-public information from purchasing or selling securities of such issuer (and options, warrants and rights relating thereto) or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the Investor to advise its Representatives about insider trading laws creates a situation where the Investor is aware of the laws but actively neglects to inform those who could be liable under them.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 78j(b) prohibits the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. While not directly mandating the provision of legal advice, actively preventing Representatives from receiving information about insider trading risks can be construed as enabling or facilitating a violation of this law, particularly if those Representatives then engage in illegal trading activities based on the Evaluation Material. This would involve aiding and abetting insider trading by knowingly and intentionally failing to provide the necessary guidance to prevent such conduct.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts securities law by removing the investor's obligation to inform its representatives about insider trading prohibitions. While 15 U.S.C. § 78j(b), as referenced in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, doesn't explicitly mandate legal advice, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation argue that preventing representatives from receiving necessary information about insider trading could facilitate a violation of this law. The original text ensured awareness of these laws, whereas the changed text creates a situation where the investor is aware but actively neglects to inform those who could be liable, creating potential liability under aiding and abetting principles.
